questions.question: What is the core idea behind a Fermi estimate?
options: Look up the exact answer in a reference table
Break the unknown into factors you can each estimate, then multiply them
Guess one number and hope it is close
Average the smallest and largest values you can imagine

explanations.A: Exact reference tables rarely exist for these questions, and looking one up defeats the purpose of a quick estimate.
explanations.B: Correct. A Fermi estimate splits the unknown into estimable factors and multiplies them, e.g. population x rate x fraction.
explanations.C: A single blind guess carries no structure and enormous error; decomposition is the whole point.
explanations.D: Averaging the extremes ignores the multiplicative structure that actually makes Fermi estimates accurate.

questions.question: To estimate the number of barbershops in a city of 1,000,000 people, which decomposition gives the count of shops?
options: (haircuts demanded per year) / (haircuts one shop supplies per year)
(city population) x (haircuts per person per year)

explanations.A: Area divided by shop size estimates how many could physically fit, not how many the demand supports.
explanations.B: Halving the population has no connection to haircut demand or shop capacity.
explanations.C: Correct. Number of shops = total annual demand / annual supply per shop, matching demand to capacity.
explanations.D: Population times haircuts per person gives total demand (haircuts), not the number of shops.

questions.question: About how many times does a human heart beat in a 75-year lifetime (roughly 70 beats per minute)?

explanations.A: A few thousand beats is reached in under an hour, far short of a lifetime.
explanations.B: A million beats happens in about ten days, not a lifetime.
explanations.C: Correct. 70/min x 60 x 24 x 365 x 75 is about 2.8 x 10^9, on the order of 10^9.
explanations.D: A trillion overshoots by roughly three orders of magnitude.

questions.question: Roughly how many seconds are in a 75-year human lifetime?

explanations.A: Correct. 75 x 3.15 x 10^7 seconds/year is about 2.4 x 10^9 seconds.
explanations.B: A million seconds is only about 11.6 days.
explanations.C: A trillion seconds is roughly 31,700 years, far too long.
explanations.D: Ten thousand seconds is under three hours.

questions.question: About how many breaths does a person take in a single day (around 15 breaths per minute)?

explanations.A: Hundreds of breaths is reached within about half an hour, not a full day.
explanations.B: Correct. 15 x 60 x 24 is about 21,600, on the order of 10^4.
explanations.C: A million breaths would take roughly 46 days at this rate.
explanations.D: A hundred million breaths is wildly beyond a single day.

questions.question: Estimate how many golf balls fit inside a standard school bus. Which order of magnitude is most reasonable?

explanations.A: Hundreds is far too few; a single seat's footprint already holds many golf balls.
explanations.B: Tens of thousands undercounts a bus interior of tens of cubic meters.
explanations.C: Correct. Bus volume ~75 m^3 (7.5 x 10^7 cm^3) divided by ~65 cm^3 per packed golf ball is about 10^6.
explanations.D: A billion golf balls would need a volume thousands of times larger than a bus.

questions.question: Why is tracking units (people, years, cups) through a Fermi estimate important?
options: Units make the final number larger
Units catch inconsistent steps and confirm the result has the right dimension
Units are only needed for the final answer
Units have no effect on estimates

explanations.A: Units do not inflate the answer; they discipline the reasoning.
explanations.B: Correct. Carrying units through each factor exposes mismatches and ensures the answer ends in sensible units (e.g. cups/year).
explanations.C: Units must be tracked at every step, not just tacked on at the end.
explanations.D: Ignoring units is exactly how order-of-magnitude blunders sneak in.

questions.question: When you multiply two estimates written as powers of ten, the exponents:

explanations.A: Correct. 10^a x 10^b = 10^(a+b), so the exponents add.
explanations.B: Exponents subtract when you divide powers of ten, not when you multiply.
explanations.C: Multiplying the exponents (a x b) is a common confusion but is incorrect.
explanations.D: Exponents change unless one factor is 10^0 = 1.

questions.question: Estimate the annual number of cups of coffee consumed in the U.S. (population ~3.3 x 10^8, about 1 cup per person per day).

explanations.A: 10^8 is only the population; it ignores the 365 days per year factor.
explanations.B: Correct. 3.3 x 10^8 people x 3.65 x 10^2 days x 1 cup is about 1.2 x 10^11.
explanations.C: 10^14 overshoots by about three orders of magnitude.
explanations.D: 10^5 is smaller than the population itself, far too low.

questions.question: Estimate the number of words an average adult speaks in one day. Which magnitude is most defensible?

explanations.A: Tens of words is exhausted in a single sentence.
explanations.B: Correct. At ~125 words/min for a few hours of talking, totals land near 10^4 words per day.
explanations.C: Tens of millions of words a day would require speaking nonstop at impossible speeds.
explanations.D: 10^10 words per day is physically impossible for one person.

questions.question: The number 7.2 x 10^6 has which order of magnitude?

explanations.A: 10^5 would describe numbers near a few hundred thousand, not 7.2 million.
explanations.B: Correct. 7.2 x 10^6 is on the order of 10^6 (millions); the leading power of ten is 6.
explanations.C: 10^7 describes numbers near 10 million; 7.2 million is below that.
explanations.D: 10^8 is a hundred million, far above 7.2 million.

questions.question: Estimate how many pizzas are eaten in the U.S. per day (population ~3.3 x 10^8, suppose ~1 slice per person per day, 8 slices per pizza).

explanations.A: 10^4 is far below even one pizza per thousand people.
explanations.B: Correct. 3.3 x 10^8 slices / 8 slices per pizza is about 4 x 10^7, on the order of 10^7.
explanations.C: 10^10 exceeds the population by two orders of magnitude.
explanations.D: 10^2 (hundreds) is absurdly small for a nation.

questions.question: If a quantity grows by a factor of 1000, by how many orders of magnitude does it increase?

explanations.A: A factor of 10 is one order of magnitude, not 1000.
explanations.B: A factor of 100 is two orders of magnitude.
explanations.C: Correct. 1000 = 10^3, which is three orders of magnitude.
explanations.D: Ten orders of magnitude would be a factor of 10^10, far larger than 1000.

questions.question: Estimate the number of hairs on a typical human head. Which order of magnitude is best?

explanations.A: Hundreds of hairs would look nearly bald.
explanations.B: Correct. A human scalp holds roughly 100,000 hairs, on the order of 10^5.
explanations.C: 10^8 hairs is a thousand times too many for one head.
explanations.D: 10^11 exceeds the number of stars some estimates give for the galaxy.

questions.question: Roughly how many minutes are in one (non-leap) year?

explanations.A: Correct. 365 x 24 x 60 = 525,600, about 5.3 x 10^5 minutes.
explanations.B: 5 x 10^3 (5000) minutes is only about 3.5 days.
explanations.C: 5 x 10^7 is closer to the number of seconds in a year, not minutes.
explanations.D: 5 x 10^9 overshoots minutes-per-year by four orders of magnitude.

questions.question: A good first step when estimating 'how many X per year' for a population is to estimate:
options: a per-person (or per-household) rate, then scale by population

explanations.A: The exact statistic is what you are approximating; it is usually unavailable.
explanations.B: Correct. Estimating a per-capita rate and multiplying by population is the standard, unit-consistent decomposition.
explanations.C: Using only the maximum gives a loose upper bound, not a central estimate.
explanations.D: A single random total abandons the structure that controls the error.

questions.question: Estimate how many cars pass through a busy intersection in one hour during the day. Which magnitude is most reasonable?

explanations.A: A few cars per hour describes a deserted rural road, not a busy intersection.
explanations.B: Correct. Roughly 10-20 cars per minute over 60 minutes gives about 10^3 per hour.
explanations.C: A million cars per hour is over 250 per second, physically impossible.
explanations.D: 10^9 cars per hour exceeds the number of cars on Earth.

questions.question: Dividing 6 x 10^9 by 3 x 10^3 gives:

explanations.B: Adding the exponents instead of subtracting wrongly gives 10^12.
explanations.C: The coefficient is 6/3 = 2, not 3.
explanations.D: Subtracting the wrong way (3 - 9) would give 10^(-6), and 2 x 10^3 is simply incorrect.

questions.question: Estimate the total mass of water (in kg) in a full Olympic swimming pool (about 50 m x 25 m x 2 m).

explanations.A: 10^3 kg is one cubic meter of water; the pool holds thousands of cubic meters.
explanations.B: Correct. Volume ~2500 m^3 x 1000 kg/m^3 = 2.5 x 10^6 kg.
explanations.C: 10^9 kg would be a pool a thousand times larger.
explanations.D: 10^1 kg is about a bucket of water, far too little.

questions.question: Which of these is closest to one billion (10^9)?
options: The number of seconds in 31.7 years
The number of seconds in a day
The number of seconds in an hour
The number of seconds in a century

explanations.A: Correct. One year is ~3.15 x 10^7 s, so 10^9 / 3.15 x 10^7 is about 31.7 years.
explanations.B: A day is only 86,400 seconds (about 10^5).
explanations.C: An hour is 3600 seconds (about 10^3.6).
explanations.D: A century is about 3.15 x 10^9 seconds, roughly three billion, not one.

questions.question: Estimate how many gallons of gasoline a typical car uses per year (12,000 miles/year, 25 miles/gallon).

explanations.A: Correct. 12,000 miles / 25 miles per gallon = 480 gallons per year.
explanations.B: 48 gallons is off by a factor of ten; check the division.
explanations.C: 4800 gallons multiplies instead of divides by mileage.
explanations.D: 5 gallons is roughly one tank, not a year of driving.

questions.question: When an estimate requires guessing a fraction (e.g. fraction of homes with a piano), a sensible habit is to:
options: always assume the fraction is exactly 1/2
pick a plausible round fraction and note it as an assumption
treat the fraction as zero to be safe

explanations.A: Defaulting to 1/2 is rarely justified and can be wildly off for rare items.
explanations.B: Correct. Choosing a defensible round fraction and stating the assumption keeps the estimate transparent and adjustable.
explanations.C: A fraction of zero forces the whole estimate to zero, which is clearly wrong.
explanations.D: Dropping the fraction overcounts by assuming everyone qualifies.

questions.question: An estimate that is 'within an order of magnitude' of the truth is off by at most a factor of about:

explanations.A: A factor of 1.1 is 10% accuracy, far stricter than order-of-magnitude.
explanations.B: Correct. Within one order of magnitude means within a factor of about 10.
explanations.C: A factor of 100 is two orders of magnitude.
explanations.D: A factor of a million is six orders of magnitude, essentially useless.

questions.question: Estimate how many times a typical clock's minute hand passes the 12 in a 24-hour day.

explanations.A: 12 would be the count for a 12-hour span, not 24 hours.
explanations.B: Correct. The minute hand completes one loop per hour, so 24 loops pass the 12 in a day.
explanations.C: 60 is the number of minute marks, not loops per day.
explanations.D: 1440 is the number of minutes in a day, not laps of the minute hand.

questions.question: Estimate the number of public elementary schools in a U.S. city of 500,000 people (assume ~7% are elementary-age children and ~400 students per school).

explanations.A: Correct. 500,000 x 0.07 = 35,000 children; 35,000 / 400 is about 88, roughly 90 schools.
explanations.B: 9 schools would need ~3900 students each, far above the assumption.
explanations.C: 900 schools implies ~39 students per school, far too few.
explanations.D: 9000 schools is more than one per 56 residents, impossible.

questions.question: Quick mental math: 20% of 350 is:

explanations.A: Correct. 10% of 350 is 35, and 20% is double that: 70.
explanations.B: 35 is only 10% of 350.
explanations.D: 700 is 200% of 350; you misplaced the decimal.

questions.question: What is 25% of 88?

explanations.A: 11 is 12.5% (one eighth) of 88, not a quarter.
explanations.B: 44 is 50% of 88, double the correct amount.
explanations.C: Correct. 25% is one quarter, and 88 / 4 = 22.

questions.question: Use the difference-of-squares trick to compute 49 x 51:

explanations.B: 2500 is 50^2; you forgot to subtract the 1.

questions.question: Fast way to do 18 x 5: multiply by 10 then halve. The result is:

explanations.A: 95 is not 18 x 5; recheck 180 / 2.
explanations.B: Correct. 18 x 10 = 180, and half of 180 is 90.
explanations.C: 80 would be 16 x 5, not 18 x 5.
explanations.D: 45 halves 90 a second time; you only halve once.

questions.question: Expressed as a percent, 1/8 equals:

explanations.B: 8% comes from reading the denominator as the percent, which is wrong.
explanations.C: 18% has no basis; 1/8 is 0.125, not 0.18.
explanations.D: 0.125% is off by a factor of 100 from 12.5%.

questions.question: What is 3/4 of 60?

explanations.A: 80 is larger than 60, impossible for a fraction less than 1.
explanations.C: Correct. 60 / 4 = 15, and 15 x 3 = 45.

questions.question: The square root of 150 is closest to:

explanations.A: 11^2 = 121, which is below 150.
explanations.B: Correct. 12^2 = 144 and 12.5^2 = 156, so sqrt(150) is about 12.2.
explanations.C: 13.5^2 is about 182, above 150.
explanations.D: 50 is 150 / 3, not its square root.

questions.question: A price rises 10% one month and falls 10% the next. The net change is:

explanations.A: Correct. 1.10 x 0.90 = 0.99, a 1% net decline.
explanations.B: Percent changes are multiplicative, so they do not cancel exactly.
explanations.C: Down 10% would require the second drop to be much larger.
explanations.D: The product is below 1, so the net change is a decrease, not an increase.

questions.question: A $100 item gets 20% off, then an extra 25% off the reduced price. Final price?

explanations.A: $55 adds the discounts to 45%, but successive discounts multiply, not add.
explanations.B: $65 does not match either order of applying the discounts.
explanations.C: Correct. 100 x 0.80 x 0.75 = 60, so the final price is $60.
explanations.D: $40 would be a single 60% discount, more than was applied.

questions.question: Compute 16 x 25 quickly (25 = 100 / 4):

explanations.A: Correct. 16 x 100 = 1600, divided by 4 is 400.
explanations.B: 4000 is off by a factor of ten.
explanations.C: 40 is off by a factor of ten the other way.
explanations.D: 320 would be 16 x 20, not 16 x 25.

questions.question: What is 0.5% of 2000?

explanations.A: 100 is 5% of 2000; you dropped a decimal place.
explanations.C: 1 is 0.05% of 2000, ten times too small.

questions.question: Compute 9 x 99 using 99 = 100 - 1:

explanations.A: 900 is 9 x 100; you must still subtract 9 x 1.
explanations.B: 909 adds 9 instead of subtracting it.

questions.question: Which is larger, 5/8 or 0.6?

explanations.A: Correct. 5/8 = 0.625, which is greater than 0.6.
explanations.B: 0.6 is smaller than 0.625.
explanations.C: 0.625 does not equal 0.6, so they are not equal.
explanations.D: Both are exact numbers, so the comparison is fully determined.

questions.question: Estimate 1234 + 5678. The sum is closest to:

explanations.A: About 5900 undercounts; 1200 + 5700 alone exceeds 6800.
explanations.B: Correct. 1234 + 5678 = 6912, closest to 6900.
explanations.C: About 7900 overshoots the true sum by roughly 1000.
explanations.D: 12,000 roughly doubles the larger number instead of adding.

questions.question: What is 12.5% of 64?

explanations.A: Correct. 12.5% is one eighth, and 64 / 8 = 8.
explanations.B: 16 is 25% of 64, double the correct value.
explanations.C: 4 is 6.25% of 64, half of what is asked.
explanations.D: 12.5 confuses the percent figure with the resulting value.

questions.question: As a simple fraction, 0.375 equals:

explanations.B: 1/3 is about 0.333, not 0.375.

questions.question: A team wins 18 of its 24 games. Its win rate is:

explanations.A: 60% would be 14.4 wins out of 24, fewer than 18.
explanations.C: 70% is 16.8 of 24, below 18.
explanations.D: 80% is 19.2 of 24, above 18.

questions.question: What is 7! (seven factorial)?

explanations.B: 720 is 6!, one factor short.
explanations.C: 40320 is 8!, one factor too many.
explanations.D: Correct. 7! = 7 x 6 x 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 5040.

questions.question: Estimate 396 / 4:

explanations.A: Correct. 400 / 4 = 100, minus 4 / 4 = 1, gives 99.
explanations.B: 90 is too low; that would be 360 / 4.
explanations.C: 100 is 400 / 4; you must subtract for the 4 you removed.
explanations.D: 79 is far below; recheck the division.

questions.question: What is 2^10?

explanations.A: 100 confuses 2^10 with 10^2.
explanations.B: 512 is 2^9, one doubling short.
explanations.D: 2048 is 2^11, one doubling too many.

questions.question: What is 30% of 30% of 1000?

explanations.A: 180 does not match 0.30 x 0.30 x 1000.
explanations.C: 300 is just 30% of 1000, applying the percent only once.

questions.question: After a 25% discount, you pay what fraction of the original price?

explanations.A: Correct. A 25% discount means you pay 100% - 25% = 75% = 3/4.
explanations.B: 1/4 is the discount removed, not the amount paid.
explanations.C: 1/2 would be a 50% discount.
explanations.D: 1/3 has no relation to a 25% discount.

questions.question: The square root of 2 is closest to:

explanations.A: 1.21 squared is about 1.46, below 2.
explanations.B: Correct. sqrt(2) is about 1.414, since 1.414^2 is about 2.
explanations.C: 1.73 is approximately sqrt(3), not sqrt(2).
explanations.D: 2.00 squared is 4, far above 2.

questions.question: What is log base 10 of 1000?

explanations.A: 1 would mean 10^1 = 1000, which is false.
explanations.D: 1000 confuses the logarithm with the argument.

questions.question: Using the Rule of 72, money growing at 7% per year roughly doubles in about how many years?

explanations.A: Correct. 72 / 7 is about 10.3 years to double.
explanations.B: 7 years confuses the growth rate with the doubling time.
explanations.C: 36 years would be the doubling time at about 2% growth.
explanations.D: 72 years would correspond to roughly 1% growth.

questions.question: Three fair coins are flipped. What is P(at least one head)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(no head) = (1/2)^3 = 1/8, so P(at least one head) = 1 - 1/8 = 7/8.
explanations.B: 1/8 is P(all tails), the complement event, not the answer.
explanations.C: 3/8 is P(exactly one head), a narrower event.
explanations.D: 1/2 ignores that getting zero heads in three flips is unlikely.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled 3 times. What is P(at least one 1)?

explanations.A: 125/216 = (5/6)^3 is P(no 1 at all), the complement.
explanations.B: 1/216 is P(all three rolls are 1).
explanations.D: 1/2 is a guess; the exact complement gives 91/216.

questions.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. What is P(even)?

explanations.A: 1/3 would be only two favorable faces; there are three even faces.
explanations.B: Correct. The even faces {2, 4, 6} are 3 of 6 outcomes, so 1/2.
explanations.C: 1/6 is the chance of a single specific face.
explanations.D: 2/3 overcounts; only half the faces are even.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 4 times. What is P(at least one tail)?

explanations.A: 1/16 is P(all heads), the complement event.
explanations.B: 1/2 ignores how unlikely four straight heads are.
explanations.C: 1/4 has no basis for this 'at least one' calculation.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is P(heart)?

explanations.A: Correct. There are 13 hearts in 52 cards: 13/52 = 1/4.
explanations.B: 1/13 is the chance of a specific rank, not a suit.
explanations.C: 1/2 would require 26 hearts.
explanations.D: 1/52 is the chance of one specific card.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(sum = 7)?

explanations.A: 1/12 (3/36) undercounts the ways to make 7.
explanations.B: 5/36 is the count for a sum of 6 or of 8, not 7.
explanations.C: Correct. There are 6 ways to roll a 7 out of 36, so 6/36 = 1/6.
explanations.D: 7/36 mistakes the target sum for the number of favorable outcomes.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(sum = 2)?

explanations.A: Correct. Only (1,1) gives a sum of 2, so 1/36.
explanations.B: 1/18 (2/36) would require two ways to make 2.
explanations.C: 1/6 vastly overstates this rarest sum.
explanations.D: 2/36 counts two outcomes, but only one produces a sum of 2.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(doubles), i.e. both dice show the same number?

explanations.A: 1/36 is the chance of one specific double like (3,3).
explanations.B: Correct. There are 6 doubles out of 36 outcomes, so 6/36 = 1/6.
explanations.C: 1/12 (3/36) undercounts the six doubles.
explanations.D: 1/3 overcounts; only six outcomes are doubles.

questions.question: Two independent events each have probability 0.1. What is P(at least one occurs)?

explanations.A: 0.20 adds the probabilities and double-counts the overlap.
explanations.B: 0.01 is P(both occur), not P(at least one).
explanations.D: 0.10 is the chance of a single event, ignoring the second.

questions.question: A bag holds 3 red and 2 blue marbles. One is drawn at random. What is P(red)?

explanations.A: Correct. 3 red out of 5 total marbles gives 3/5.
explanations.B: 2/5 is P(blue), not P(red).
explanations.C: 3/2 exceeds 1 and cannot be a probability.
explanations.D: 1/2 ignores that red marbles outnumber blue.

questions.question: Two fair coins are flipped. What is P(both heads)?

explanations.A: 1/2 is the chance for a single coin, not two.
explanations.B: Correct. (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4 for two independent heads.
explanations.C: 1/3 wrongly treats {HH, HT/TH, TT} as equally likely groups.
explanations.D: 3/4 is P(at least one head), not both heads.

questions.question: Two cards are drawn without replacement from a 52-card deck. What is P(both aces)?

explanations.A: 1/169 = (4/52)^2 assumes replacement, which the problem excludes.
explanations.B: 1/16 ignores the deck composition entirely.
explanations.C: 2/52 is unrelated to drawing two aces.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled 4 times. What is P(at least one 6)? (Use the complement.)

explanations.B: About 0.48 is (5/6)^4, the probability of no 6.
explanations.C: About 0.67 overstates the chance.
explanations.D: About 0.33 understates it; the four rolls give better than even odds.

questions.question: A spinner has 8 equal sections numbered 1-8. What is P(number greater than 5)?

explanations.A: 1/2 would be four favorable sections, but only three exceed 5.
explanations.B: Correct. The values {6, 7, 8} are 3 of 8 sections, so 3/8.
explanations.C: 5/8 counts the numbers 5 or below by mistake.
explanations.D: 1/4 (2/8) undercounts the three qualifying sections.

questions.question: Independent events have P(A) = 0.5 and P(B) = 0.4. What is P(A and B)?

explanations.A: 0.90 adds the probabilities, which gives a union upper bound, not the intersection.
explanations.B: 0.10 is the difference of the probabilities, not their product.
explanations.C: Correct. For independent events, P(A and B) = 0.5 x 0.4 = 0.20.
explanations.D: 0.45 averages the two probabilities, which is not how intersection works.

questions.question: A player makes 80% of free throws. What is P(missing at least one of 3 attempts)?

explanations.A: 0.20 is the chance of missing a single attempt, not 'at least one of three'.
explanations.B: 0.512 is P(making all three), the complement event.
explanations.C: 0.60 has no basis in this calculation.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(at least one die is even)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(both odd) = (1/2)^2 = 1/4, so P(at least one even) = 3/4.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the chance a single die is even, not the two-dice event.
explanations.C: 1/4 is P(both odd), the complement.
explanations.D: 1 would mean an even die is guaranteed, which is false.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled. What is P(rolling a prime number)?

explanations.A: 1/3 counts only two primes, but there are three.
explanations.B: Correct. The primes on a die are {2, 3, 5}, which is 3 of 6, so 1/2.
explanations.C: 2/3 likely counts 1 as prime, but 1 is not prime.
explanations.D: 1/6 is the chance of a single specific face.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped until the first head. What is P(the first head appears on the 2nd flip)?

explanations.A: 1/2 is the chance the first head appears on the very first flip.
explanations.B: 1/8 is the chance the first head appears on the 3rd flip.
explanations.C: Correct. Tail then head: (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4.
explanations.D: 3/4 exceeds the chance for any single specific stopping time here.

questions.question: Two dice are rolled 24 times. What is P(at least one double-six)? (A classic gambling puzzle.)

explanations.A: Correct. 1 - (35/36)^24 is about 0.491, just under even odds.
explanations.B: About 0.51 is (35/36)^24, the chance of no double-six.
explanations.C: About 0.67 overstates the probability.
explanations.D: About 1.0 wrongly assumes a double-six is nearly certain in 24 throws.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard deck. What is P(face card: J, Q, or K)?

explanations.A: 1/4 (13/52) counts a full suit, not the 12 face cards.
explanations.B: Correct. There are 12 face cards in 52: 12/52 = 3/13.
explanations.C: 1/13 is the chance of one specific rank like all jacks-only miscount.
explanations.D: 12/13 inverts the fraction; face cards are a minority of the deck.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(the sum is even)?

explanations.A: Correct. A sum is even when both dice are even or both odd, which is 18 of 36 outcomes, so 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/3 undercounts the even-sum outcomes.
explanations.C: 2/3 overcounts; even and odd sums are equally likely.
explanations.D: 1/4 is far too small for an even sum.

questions.question: Three fair coins are flipped. What is P(all three match, i.e. all heads or all tails)?

explanations.A: 1/8 counts only one matching outcome (e.g. HHH), not both.
explanations.B: 1/2 vastly overstates the chance of all three matching.
explanations.C: 3/8 is the chance of exactly one head (or one tail), a different event.
explanations.D: Correct. HHH and TTT are 2 of 8 outcomes, so 2/8 = 1/4.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is P(sum is at least 10)?

explanations.A: 1/12 (3/36) counts only the sums of 10, missing 11 and 12.
explanations.B: 1/9 (4/36) still undercounts the qualifying outcomes.
explanations.C: Correct. Sums of 10, 11, 12 occur in 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 ways, so 6/36 = 1/6.
explanations.D: 1/4 (9/36) overcounts the high-sum outcomes.

questions.question: A factory's defect rate is 2%. In a random sample of 5 items, what is P(at least one defective)?

explanations.A: 0.02 is the chance a single item is defective, not the sample.
explanations.B: Correct. 1 - (0.98)^5 is about 1 - 0.904 = 0.096, roughly 0.10.
explanations.C: About 0.90 is (0.98)^5, the chance of zero defectives.
explanations.D: 0.50 has no basis; defects are rare, so the chance is well under half.

questions.question: Three fair dice are rolled and summed. What is the expected value of the total?

explanations.A: 7 is the expected sum of two dice, not three.
explanations.B: Correct. By linearity, E[sum] = 3 x 3.5 = 10.5.
explanations.C: 3.5 is the expectation of a single die.
explanations.D: 21 is the maximum possible total, not the average.

questions.question: A game pays $10 with probability 0.3 and $0 otherwise. What is its expected value?

explanations.A: $10 is the prize, not the probability-weighted average.
explanations.B: $7 weights by the losing probability instead of the winning one.
explanations.D: $0.30 confuses the probability with a dollar amount.

questions.question: You pay $2 to play. You win $5 with probability 0.5, else nothing. What is your expected net result?

explanations.A: Correct. Expected winnings are 0.5 x $5 = $2.50; minus the $2 cost gives +$0.50.
explanations.B: -$0.50 has the sign wrong; the game is favorable.
explanations.C: +$2.50 forgets to subtract the $2 cost to play.
explanations.D: $0 would require expected winnings to equal the $2 cost exactly.

questions.question: If E[X] = 4, what is E[3X + 2]?

explanations.A: 6 only adds 2 to E[X] and ignores the factor of 3.
explanations.B: 12 is 3 x E[X] but forgets the +2 constant.
explanations.C: 4 is E[X] itself, before the transformation.
explanations.D: Correct. E[3X + 2] = 3 x 4 + 2 = 14.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled 6 times. What is the expected number of 6s?

explanations.A: Correct. By linearity of indicators, E[count] = 6 x (1/6) = 1.
explanations.B: 6 would require a 6 on every roll.
explanations.C: 1/6 is the chance of a 6 on a single roll, not the expected count over six.
explanations.D: 36 confuses the count with the number of die-face pairs.

questions.question: Two players each draw one card from a 52-card deck (no replacement). By symmetry, what is P(player 1's card outranks player 2's)? (Ties are possible by rank.)

explanations.A: 1/2 ignores that the two cards can tie in rank, which removes some probability.
explanations.B: 1/17 is P(a rank tie), since 3 of the remaining 51 cards match player 1's rank.
explanations.C: Correct. P(tie) = 3/51 = 1/17; by symmetry the remaining 16/17 splits evenly, giving 8/17.
explanations.D: 16/17 is the chance of no tie, not the chance player 1 is higher.

questions.question: Five equally-matched runners race. What is P(a specific runner finishes first)?

explanations.A: 1/2 would only make sense with two runners.
explanations.B: Correct. By symmetry each of the 5 runners is equally likely to be first: 1/5.
explanations.C: 1/4 would correspond to four runners.
explanations.D: 1/120 is 1/5!, the chance of one specific full ordering.

questions.question: Alice and Bob alternately flip a fair coin, Alice first; the first head wins. What is P(Alice wins)?

explanations.A: Correct. With p = P(Alice wins), p = 1/2 + (1/4)p gives p = 2/3.
explanations.B: 1/2 ignores Alice's first-move advantage.
explanations.C: 1/3 is Bob's win probability.
explanations.D: 3/4 overstates the first-mover edge.

questions.question: Two fair dice (A and B) are rolled. What is P(die A shows strictly more than die B)?

explanations.A: 1/2 ignores the chance of a tie, which uses up some probability.
explanations.B: 1/6 is P(a tie), since the dice match in 6 of 36 outcomes.
explanations.C: 7/12 overstates it; it would leave too little for ties and the reverse.
explanations.D: Correct. P(tie) = 1/6, so the remaining 5/6 splits evenly: (5/6)/2 = 5/12.

questions.question: A $1 lottery ticket wins $500 with probability 1/1000. What is the expected net value of buying one ticket?

explanations.A: +$0.50 is the expected payout but forgets to subtract the $1 cost.
explanations.B: Correct. Expected payout is 500/1000 = $0.50; minus the $1 ticket gives -$0.50.
explanations.C: -$1.00 ignores the expected payout entirely.
explanations.D: +$499 is the net only in the rare winning case, not the expectation.

questions.question: Linearity of expectation, E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y], holds when X and Y are:

explanations.A: Independence is required for products, not for the sum's expectation.
explanations.B: The variables need not share a distribution.
explanations.C: Correct. Linearity of expectation always holds, regardless of dependence.
explanations.D: It holds for all random variables with finite expectations.

questions.question: Roll a fair die. If the face is even you receive that many dollars; if odd you receive $0. What is the expected payout?

explanations.A: Correct. (2 + 4 + 6)/6 = 12/6 = $2.00, since odd rolls pay nothing.
explanations.B: $3.50 is the plain die mean, ignoring that odd rolls pay $0.
explanations.C: $1.75 halves the result incorrectly.
explanations.D: $6.00 is the best case, not the average.

questions.question: From 100 people, two are chosen in order at random. What is P(you are chosen first AND your friend second)?

explanations.A: 1/100 is only the chance you are chosen first.
explanations.B: 1/200 has no basis in the sequential selection.
explanations.C: 1/10000 = (1/100)^2 assumes selection with replacement.
explanations.D: Correct. (1/100)(1/99) = 1/9900 without replacement.

questions.question: What is the expected number of flips of a fair coin needed to get the first head?

explanations.A: 1 would mean the first flip is always a head.
explanations.B: Correct. For a geometric distribution, E = 1/p = 1/0.5 = 2.
explanations.C: 0.5 is the success probability, not the expected wait.
explanations.D: 4 would correspond to p = 1/4, not a fair coin.

questions.question: What is the expected value of a single roll of a fair 4-sided die (faces 1-4)?

explanations.A: 2 is below the true average of the four faces.
explanations.B: 3 is above the midpoint of 1 through 4.
explanations.D: 4 is the maximum face, not the mean.

questions.question: A deck is shuffled uniformly at random. What is P(the ace of spades is the top card)?

explanations.A: Correct. By symmetry every card is equally likely on top: 1/52.
explanations.B: 1/13 is the chance of a given rank, not a specific card.
explanations.C: 1/4 is the chance of a given suit.
explanations.D: 1/2 has no basis for a single specific card.

questions.question: A deck is shuffled at random. What is P(the ace of spades lands in the top half, the first 26 cards)?

explanations.A: 1/26 is the chance of one specific position, not the top 26.
explanations.B: Correct. By symmetry the card is equally likely in any of 52 positions; 26/52 = 1/2.
explanations.C: 1/4 understates the half-deck region.
explanations.D: 1/52 is a single position, not a range of 26.

questions.question: Thirteen cards are dealt from a 52-card deck. What is the expected number of aces in the hand?

explanations.A: Correct. E = 13 x (4/52) = 13/13 = 1 by linearity of expectation.
explanations.B: 4 would mean all aces are always dealt.
explanations.C: 13 is the hand size, not the ace count.
explanations.D: 0.25 is the per-card chance of an ace, not the expected total.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 10 times. Given P(exactly 5 heads) is about 0.246, what is P(more heads than tails)?

explanations.A: 0.50 ignores the substantial chance of an exact 5-5 tie.
explanations.B: 0.246 is P(a tie), not P(more heads).
explanations.C: Correct. By symmetry, (1 - 0.246)/2 is about 0.377.
explanations.D: About 0.62 double-counts; it omits dividing the non-tie probability in half.

questions.question: A biased coin lands heads with probability 0.7. What is the expected number of heads in 10 flips?

explanations.A: 5 would be the expectation only for a fair coin.
explanations.B: 10 would require heads on every flip.
explanations.C: 3 is the expected number of tails, not heads.
explanations.D: Correct. E = 10 x 0.7 = 7.

questions.question: Two people are seated at random in a row of 3 chairs. What is P(they sit next to each other)?

explanations.A: Correct. Of the C(3,2) = 3 possible seat pairs, 2 are adjacent, so 2/3.
explanations.B: 1/3 is the chance they are NOT adjacent (the end seats).
explanations.C: 1/2 does not match the three equally likely seat pairs.
explanations.D: 1 would mean they always sit together, which is false.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the expected value of the larger of the two faces (ties count that value)?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the mean of a single die, but the max skews higher.
explanations.B: Correct. Sum of k(2k-1)/36 over k=1..6 equals 161/36, about 4.47.
explanations.C: 6 is the largest possible max, not the average max.
explanations.D: 7 is the expected sum, not the expected maximum.

questions.question: A coin pays $1 for heads and $0 for tails, played 100 times with a fair coin. What is the expected total?

explanations.A: $100 would require heads every single time.
explanations.B: $1 is the payout per win, not the expected total.
explanations.D: $25 would correspond to a heads probability of 0.25.

questions.question: The letters A, B, C are arranged in random order. What is P(A appears before B)?

explanations.A: Correct. By symmetry A is equally likely before or after B, so 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/3 has no basis; C's position does not change the A-vs-B symmetry.
explanations.C: 1/6 is the chance of one specific full ordering.
explanations.D: 2/3 overstates the symmetric 50-50 outcome.

questions.question: n people toss their hats into a pile and each picks one at random. What is the expected number who get their own hat back?

explanations.A: n would mean everyone always gets their own hat.
explanations.B: Correct. Each person matches with probability 1/n; by linearity E = n x (1/n) = 1.
explanations.C: 0 ignores that matches do happen on average.
explanations.D: 1/n is one person's match chance, not the expected total.

questions.question: A card is drawn from a standard deck. What is P(heart | the card is red)?

explanations.A: Correct. Among the 26 red cards, 13 are hearts: 13/26 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is the unconditional P(heart), ignoring that we know the card is red.
explanations.C: 13/52 is also the unconditional probability, not the conditional one.
explanations.D: 1/13 confuses a rank probability with the hearts-among-red calculation.

questions.question: A family has two children. You learn at least one is a boy. What is P(both are boys)?

explanations.A: 1/2 is the tempting trap; it ignores that {BG, GB, BB} are the three equally likely qualifying cases.
explanations.B: 1/4 is the unconditional P(both boys) before learning anything.
explanations.C: Correct. Given at least one boy, the cases BB, BG, GB are equally likely, so P(BB) = 1/3.
explanations.D: 2/3 inverts the count of favorable cases.

questions.question: A family has two children. You learn the ELDER child is a boy. What is P(both are boys)?

explanations.A: Correct. Fixing the elder as a boy leaves the younger as a 50-50 coin, so P(both boys) = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/3 is the answer to the different 'at least one boy' problem.
explanations.C: 1/4 is the unconditional probability before any information.
explanations.D: 2/3 does not match the single remaining 50-50 child.

questions.question: A disease affects 1% of people. A test is 99% sensitive and 95% specific. If you test positive, what is P(disease)?

explanations.A: 99% is the sensitivity, not the chance you are sick given a positive test.
explanations.C: 95% is the specificity, not the posterior probability.
explanations.D: 50% ignores the low 1% base rate that keeps the posterior small.

questions.question: Ignoring the base rate when interpreting a positive medical test typically leads to:

explanations.A: Correct. For a rare disease, neglecting the low base rate makes people wildly overstate P(disease | positive).
explanations.B: Dropping the prior is exactly what breaks calibration.
explanations.C: The error runs the other way: people overestimate, not underestimate.
explanations.D: The base rate strongly affects the posterior, so ignoring it matters a lot.

questions.question: If P(A and B) = 0.12 and P(B) = 0.3, what is P(A | B)?

explanations.A: 0.12 is the joint probability, not the conditional one.
explanations.B: 0.30 is P(B), the denominator, not the result.
explanations.C: 0.036 multiplies the two values instead of dividing.
explanations.D: Correct. P(A | B) = P(A and B)/P(B) = 0.12/0.3 = 0.40.

questions.question: If P(A | B) = P(A), then events A and B are:

explanations.A: Mutually exclusive events have P(A | B) = 0, not P(A).
explanations.B: Dependence would mean B changes A's probability, but here it does not.
explanations.C: Correct. P(A | B) = P(A) is precisely the definition of independence.
explanations.D: Identical events would force P(A | B) = 1, not P(A).

questions.question: Two fair coins are flipped. You are told at least one shows heads. What is P(both heads)?

explanations.A: Correct. The qualifying outcomes HH, HT, TH are equally likely, so P(HH) = 1/3.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the intuitive trap that forgets HT and TH are two distinct cases.
explanations.C: 1/4 is the unconditional P(both heads).
explanations.D: 2/3 inverts the favorable count.

questions.question: 1 in 1000 people has a condition. The test is always positive when present and has a 5% false-positive rate. If you test positive, P(condition)?

explanations.A: 95% confuses the specificity with the posterior probability.
explanations.C: 50% ignores how rare the condition is.
explanations.D: 99% wildly overstates the chance given the tiny base rate.

questions.question: Two cards are drawn without replacement. Given the first card was an ace, what is P(the second is also an ace)?

explanations.A: 4/52 ignores that one ace is already gone and the deck has shrunk.
explanations.B: 1/13 is the unconditional ace probability for a full deck.
explanations.C: 3/52 uses the right numerator but the wrong (full-deck) denominator.
explanations.D: Correct. After removing one ace, 3 aces remain in 51 cards: 3/51 = 1/17.

questions.question: On 70% of days it is cloudy, and on cloudy days it rains 50% of the time. What is P(cloudy AND rain)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(cloudy) x P(rain | cloudy) = 0.7 x 0.5 = 0.35.
explanations.B: 0.50 is the conditional rain probability, not the joint event.
explanations.C: 0.70 is just P(cloudy).
explanations.D: 1.20 adds the probabilities, exceeding 1, which is impossible.

questions.question: One coin is fair, the other has heads on both sides. You pick one at random, flip it, and get heads. What is P(you picked the two-headed coin)?

explanations.A: 1/2 is the prior, before observing the heads.
explanations.B: 1/3 inverts the Bayesian update.
explanations.D: 1 would ignore that the fair coin can also show heads.

questions.question: Box A has 2 gold and 1 silver coin; Box B has 1 gold and 2 silver. You pick a box at random and draw a gold coin. What is P(it was Box A)?

explanations.B: 1/2 is the prior before seeing the gold coin.
explanations.C: 1/3 is P(Box B | gold), the complement.
explanations.D: 3/4 does not match the Bayesian update.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Are the events 'first die shows 6' and 'the sum is 7' independent?

explanations.A: Correct. P(first=6) = 1/6, P(sum=7) = 1/6, and P(both) = P(6,1) = 1/36 = (1/6)(1/6), so they are independent.
explanations.B: They are not mutually exclusive; (6,1) satisfies both.
explanations.C: The joint probability equals the product, so there is no positive dependence.
explanations.D: The probabilities are fully specified, so independence can be checked directly.

questions.question: 20% of emails are spam. The word 'free' appears in 60% of spam and 5% of non-spam. If an email contains 'free', what is P(spam)?

explanations.A: 0.60 is the likelihood P('free' | spam), not the posterior.
explanations.C: 0.20 is the prior, before seeing the word 'free'.
explanations.D: 0.50 ignores the actual likelihood ratio of the word.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled and the sum is 8. What is P(at least one die shows a 5)?

explanations.A: 1/6 is the unconditional chance a die shows 5, ignoring the sum=8 condition.
explanations.B: 1/5 counts only one favorable outcome among the five.
explanations.C: Correct. Sum 8 has outcomes (2,6),(3,5),(4,4),(5,3),(6,2); two of these include a 5, so 2/5.
explanations.D: 1/2 overcounts the outcomes containing a 5.

questions.question: The 'prosecutor's fallacy' confuses P(evidence | innocent) with:

explanations.A: Correct. The fallacy treats the small P(evidence | innocent) as if it were P(innocent | evidence), which requires the base rate.
explanations.B: P(evidence) is the normalizing term, not what is being confused.
explanations.C: P(guilty) alone is not the conditional being swapped.
explanations.D: The prior is an ingredient in Bayes' rule, not the quantity confused with the likelihood.

questions.question: A card is drawn. What is P(red | the card is a king)?

explanations.A: Correct. Of the 4 kings, 2 are red, so 2/4 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/26 is the chance of one specific red card, not this conditional.
explanations.C: 2/52 is the unconditional chance of a red king.
explanations.D: 1/13 ignores that we have already conditioned on the card being a king.

questions.question: When two independent tests both come back positive, you update your belief by:

explanations.A: Averaging probabilities is not how Bayesian updates combine evidence.
explanations.B: Correct. Independent evidence multiplies the likelihood ratios applied to the prior odds.
explanations.C: Ignoring the second test discards real information.
explanations.D: Subtracting probabilities has no basis in Bayes' rule.

questions.question: A Monty Hall variant: the host opens a remaining door at RANDOM (not knowing the prize) and it happens to reveal a goat. Now what is P(winning by switching)?

explanations.A: 2/3 is the answer only when the host knowingly avoids the prize; a random reveal is different.
explanations.B: Correct. With an uninformed host who happened to reveal a goat, the two remaining doors are equally likely, so switching wins 1/2.
explanations.C: 1/3 understates the updated probability after the random reveal.
explanations.D: 1 would mean switching always wins, which is false.

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.5, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A and B) = 0.25, what is P(A or B)?

explanations.A: 1.00 adds the probabilities without subtracting the overlap.
explanations.B: 0.25 is the intersection, not the union.
explanations.C: 0.50 is a single event's probability, not the union.

questions.question: A hypothesis has prior probability 0.1%. New evidence has a likelihood ratio of 50 in its favor. The posterior probability is closest to:

explanations.A: Correct. Prior odds about 0.001, times 50, gives posterior odds about 0.05, i.e. roughly 5%.
explanations.B: About 50% would require far stronger evidence against such a low prior.
explanations.C: About 95% would need an enormous likelihood ratio.
explanations.D: About 0.1% ignores the substantial update from the evidence.

questions.question: Drawing items from a bag WITH replacement makes successive draws:

explanations.A: Dependence arises without replacement, when earlier draws change the composition.
explanations.B: Successive draws are not mutually exclusive events.
explanations.C: Correct. Replacing each item restores the bag, so each draw is independent of the others.
explanations.D: Drawing with replacement is entirely possible.

questions.question: Three cards: one red on both sides, one red/black, one black on both sides. You draw one, and the face shown is red. What is P(its other side is also red)?

explanations.A: Correct. Of the 3 equally likely red faces, 2 belong to the red/red card, so 2/3.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the classic trap; it counts cards rather than the equally likely red faces.
explanations.C: 1/3 inverts the favorable count.
explanations.D: 3/4 does not match the three red faces.

questions.question: Holding test sensitivity and specificity fixed, as disease prevalence increases, the positive predictive value (PPV):

explanations.A: PPV does not fall as prevalence rises; true positives become relatively more common.
explanations.B: Correct. Higher prevalence yields more true positives per false positive, raising the PPV.
explanations.C: PPV depends on prevalence, so it does not stay fixed.
explanations.D: PPV approaches 0 only as prevalence approaches 0, the opposite direction.

questions.question: How many ways can 5 distinct books be arranged on a shelf?

explanations.A: 25 is 5^2, not the number of orderings.
explanations.B: Correct. 5! = 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 120.
explanations.C: 5 counts the books, not their arrangements.
explanations.D: 3125 is 5^5, which would allow repeats in 5 slots.

questions.question: What is C(5, 2), the number of 2-element subsets of a 5-element set?

explanations.B: 20 is P(5,2), the number of ordered pairs.
explanations.C: 25 is 5^2, allowing repeats and order.

questions.question: How many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 10 people?

explanations.A: 30 is 10 x 3, which has no combinatorial meaning here.
explanations.B: 720 is P(10,3), counting ordered selections.
explanations.D: 1000 is 10^3, allowing repeats and order.

questions.question: How many 3-digit PIN codes are possible using digits 0-9 with repeats allowed?

explanations.A: 720 is P(10,3), which forbids repeats.
explanations.B: 30 is 10 x 3, not the count of codes.
explanations.C: 100 is 10^2, only two positions.
explanations.D: Correct. With repeats allowed, 10 x 10 x 10 = 1000.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in 'LEVEL'?

explanations.A: Correct. 'LEVEL' has two L's and two E's: 5!/(2!2!) = 120/4 = 30.
explanations.B: 120 is 5! and ignores the repeated letters.
explanations.C: 60 divides by only one pair of repeats.
explanations.D: 10 is far too small; it does not match the multiset count.

questions.question: How many distinct handshakes occur if 6 people all shake hands once?

explanations.A: 30 is 6 x 5, which counts each handshake twice.
explanations.B: Correct. C(6,2) = 15 distinct pairs.
explanations.C: 36 is 6^2, counting self-pairs and order.
explanations.D: 6 counts the people, not the handshakes.

questions.question: How many subsets does a set with 4 elements have (including the empty set)?

explanations.A: 8 is 2^3, the count for a 3-element set.
explanations.B: 4 counts the elements, not the subsets.
explanations.C: Correct. A 4-element set has 2^4 = 16 subsets.
explanations.D: 24 is 4!, the number of orderings, not subsets.

questions.question: How many ways can 4 people be seated around a round table, treating rotations as identical?

explanations.A: Correct. Circular arrangements of n people number (n-1)! = 3! = 6.
explanations.B: 24 = 4! counts linear arrangements, not circular ones.
explanations.C: 12 has no standard basis for this count.
explanations.D: 4 counts the seats, not the arrangements.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 5 times. How many of the 32 sequences have exactly 2 heads?

explanations.A: 20 is P(5,2), counting order of the two head positions.
explanations.B: Correct. C(5,2) = 10 choices of which two flips are heads.
explanations.C: 32 is the total number of sequences (2^5).
explanations.D: 5 counts the flips, not the 2-head sequences.

questions.question: From 5 ice-cream flavors, how many ways can you choose 2 scoops if repeats are allowed and order does not matter?

explanations.A: 10 = C(5,2) forbids choosing the same flavor twice.
explanations.B: 25 = 5^2 counts ordered selections with repeats.
explanations.C: 20 = P(5,2) counts ordered selections without repeats.
explanations.D: Correct. Combinations with repetition: C(5+2-1, 2) = C(6,2) = 15.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in 'MISSISSIPPI' (4 S, 4 I, 2 P, 1 M)?

explanations.B: 39,916,800 is 11! and ignores the repeated letters.
explanations.C: 165 is far too small for an 11-letter multiset.
explanations.D: 2520 does not match the correct factorial division.

questions.question: How many diagonals does a hexagon (6 vertices) have?

explanations.A: 6 counts the sides, not the diagonals.
explanations.B: Correct. n(n-3)/2 = 6 x 3/2 = 9 diagonals.
explanations.C: 15 = C(6,2) counts all vertex pairs, including the 6 sides.
explanations.D: 12 overcounts the diagonals.

questions.question: How many license plates have 3 letters (A-Z) followed by 3 digits (0-9), with repeats allowed?

explanations.A: 17,576 = 26^3 counts only the letter portion.
explanations.B: 1,757,600 is off by a factor of ten from the correct product.
explanations.D: 456,976 = 26^4, an extra letter and no digits.

questions.question: How many distinct 5-card hands can be dealt from a 52-card deck?

explanations.B: 311,875,200 is P(52,5), counting ordered deals.
explanations.C: 380,204,032 is 52^5, allowing repeats and order.
explanations.D: 22,100 is C(52,3), a 3-card count.

questions.question: How many ways can 3 identical candies be distributed between 2 children (each child gets 0 or more)?

explanations.A: 8 = 2^3 would treat the candies as distinct and ordered.
explanations.B: Correct. Stars and bars: C(3+2-1, 2-1) = C(4,1) = 4 (splits 0-3, 1-2, 2-1, 3-0).
explanations.C: 6 overcounts the symmetric splits.
explanations.D: 9 = 3^2 has no basis for identical candies.

questions.question: How many binary strings of length 6 are there?

explanations.A: 12 = 6 x 2 has no combinatorial meaning here.
explanations.B: 36 = 6^2 uses the wrong base and exponent.
explanations.C: 32 = 2^5 is the count for length 5.
explanations.D: Correct. Each of 6 positions has 2 choices: 2^6 = 64.

questions.question: What is P(4,2), the number of ways to arrange 2 of 4 distinct items in order?

explanations.A: Correct. P(4,2) = 4 x 3 = 12 ordered pairs.
explanations.B: 6 = C(4,2) ignores order.
explanations.C: 8 has no basis for this permutation count.
explanations.D: 24 = 4! arranges all four items.

questions.question: A pizza shop offers 8 toppings. How many distinct topping combinations are possible (any number of toppings, including none)?

explanations.A: 64 = 2^6 would be the count for 6 toppings.
explanations.B: Correct. Each topping is in or out: 2^8 = 256.
explanations.C: 40320 = 8! counts orderings, which are irrelevant for a topping set.
explanations.D: 8 counts the toppings, not their combinations.

questions.question: In how many ways can gold, silver, and bronze medals be awarded among 8 contestants?

explanations.A: 56 = C(8,3) ignores that the three medals are distinct (ordered).
explanations.B: 512 = 8^3 would let one person win multiple medals.
explanations.C: Correct. P(8,3) = 8 x 7 x 6 = 336.
explanations.D: 24 = 4! is unrelated to choosing 3 of 8 in order.

questions.question: In how many ways can 4 people be split into 2 unlabeled pairs?

explanations.A: Correct. The pairings are {AB,CD}, {AC,BD}, {AD,BC}: 3 ways.
explanations.B: 6 = C(4,2) counts a single pair, double-counting the partner pair.
explanations.C: 12 overcounts by treating the pairs as ordered.
explanations.D: 4 has no basis for unlabeled pairings.

questions.question: Four people each independently pick a number from 1 to 4. What is P(all four pick different numbers)?

explanations.A: 1/256 is the chance of one specific assignment, not all-distinct.
explanations.B: 1/4 has no basis for the all-different event.
explanations.C: 1/24 forgets the total of 4^4 equally likely outcomes.

questions.question: How many ways can 3 men and 2 women (all distinct) stand in a single row?

explanations.A: 12 undercounts the arrangements of five distinct people.
explanations.B: Correct. All 5 are distinct, so 5! = 120.
explanations.C: 10 = C(5,2) is a selection count, not an ordering.
explanations.D: 60 divides 120 by 2 without justification.

questions.question: What is the probability that a random 5-card hand is a flush (all five cards the same suit)?

explanations.A: About 0.25 confuses the chance of one suit with the rare all-same-suit event.
explanations.B: About 0.05 is still far too high for a flush.
explanations.D: About 0.5 vastly overstates how often a flush occurs.

questions.question: How many ways can 3 toppings be chosen from 6 when order does not matter and repeats are not allowed?

explanations.A: 18 = 6 x 3 has no combinatorial meaning here.
explanations.B: 120 = P(6,3) counts ordered selections.
explanations.C: 216 = 6^3 allows repeats and order.
explanations.D: Correct. C(6,3) = 20.

questions.question: How many distinct anagrams does the word 'BOOK' have?

explanations.A: Correct. 'BOOK' has two O's: 4!/2! = 24/2 = 12.
explanations.B: 24 = 4! ignores the repeated O.
explanations.C: 6 over-divides the factorial.
explanations.D: 4 counts the letters, not the arrangements.

questions.question: In the classic 3-door Monty Hall game, after the host knowingly opens a goat door, what is P(winning by switching)?

explanations.A: Correct. Your first pick stays at 1/3, so the other unopened door carries the remaining 2/3.
explanations.B: 1/2 wrongly treats the two remaining doors as equally likely after an informed reveal.
explanations.C: 1/3 is the probability of winning by staying.
explanations.D: 1 would mean switching guarantees a win, which it does not.

questions.question: A 100-door Monty Hall game: you pick 1 door, the host knowingly opens 98 goat doors. What is P(winning by switching to the last door)?

explanations.A: 1/2 ignores how unlikely your single original pick was.
explanations.B: 1/100 is the chance your original door is correct (the stay probability).
explanations.C: 98/100 miscounts; the remaining probability concentrates fully on the one unopened door.
explanations.D: Correct. Your pick stays at 1/100, so the surviving door holds 99/100.

questions.question: What is the smallest group size for which it is more likely than not that two people share a birthday?

explanations.A: 183 (about half of 365) is the intuitive but wrong guess; pairs accumulate much faster.
explanations.B: Correct. At 23 people the probability of a shared birthday first exceeds 50%.
explanations.C: 366 guarantees a shared birthday (pigeonhole), not just better-than-even odds.
explanations.D: 50 is more than needed; the 50% threshold is already crossed at 23.

questions.question: How many distinct pairs can be formed from 23 people?

explanations.A: 23 is the number of people, not pairs.
explanations.B: 46 is 2 x 23, not the count of pairs.
explanations.D: 506 = 23 x 22 counts each pair twice.

questions.question: What is the expected number of rolls of a fair die needed to first see a 6?

explanations.A: Correct. For a geometric distribution, E = 1/p = 1/(1/6) = 6.
explanations.B: 1 would mean a 6 always appears on the first roll.
explanations.C: 36 squares the expected wait without reason.
explanations.D: 3 understates the geometric mean of 6.

questions.question: You flip a fair coin: each heads pays $1 and you flip again; the first tails ends the game. What is your expected total winnings?

explanations.A: $2 double-counts; the expected number of heads before the first tail is 1, not 2.
explanations.B: Correct. The heads before the first tail form a geometric count with mean (1/2)/(1/2) = 1, so $1.
explanations.C: $0.50 is only the chance of the first flip being heads, not the expectation.
explanations.D: The payout per head is bounded at $1, so the total is finite.

questions.question: In the two-envelope problem, one envelope has twice the other. You open one with $100 and reason the other averages (50 + 200)/2 = $125, so you should always switch. The flaw is:
options: assuming $50 and $200 are equally likely without a proper prior over amounts
the $125 calculation is correct and you should always switch
switching costs nothing, so the paradox is real

explanations.A: Correct. The naive argument assumes a uniform prior that cannot exist over all amounts; with a proper prior the symmetric 'always switch' conclusion disappears.
explanations.B: If switching were always better, it would be better before opening too, which is absurd by symmetry.
explanations.C: Doubling is fine; the error is probabilistic, not physical.
explanations.D: Zero switching cost does not rescue the flawed equal-likelihood assumption.

questions.question: A fair coin has just landed heads 5 times in a row. What is P(heads) on the next flip?

explanations.A: 'Less than 1/2' is the gambler's fallacy; the coin has no memory.
explanations.B: 0 would mean heads is now impossible, which is false.
explanations.C: Correct. Independent flips are memoryless, so P(heads) stays 1/2.
explanations.D: 'More than 1/2' wrongly assumes a streak makes heads more likely.

questions.question: You win the face value (in dollars) of one fair die roll. What is the fair price to play?

explanations.A: Correct. E[face value] = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5, so a fair price is $3.50.
explanations.B: $6.00 is the maximum payout, not the expected one.
explanations.C: $1.00 is the minimum payout, far below the average.
explanations.D: $3.00 is close but below the true mean of 3.5.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled and you are paid the absolute difference |a - b| in dollars. What is the expected payout?

explanations.A: $0 would require the dice to always match.
explanations.B: $1.00 undercounts the larger differences.
explanations.C: $3.50 is the mean of a single die, unrelated to the difference.
explanations.D: Correct. Summing d x count gives 70/36, about $1.94.

questions.question: Estimate piano tuners in a city of 2 million: ~2.5 people/household, 1 in 20 households owns a piano, each piano tuned once/year, one tuner does ~1000 tunings/year. About how many tuners?

explanations.A: About 4 undercounts; recheck the number of pianos.
explanations.B: Correct. 800,000 households x (1/20) = 40,000 pianos/tunings; 40,000/1000 is about 40 tuners.
explanations.C: About 400 overcounts by a factor of ten.
explanations.D: About 4000 is the number of pianos divided by 10, not the tuners.

questions.question: Estimate the number of fast-food restaurants in a city of 1 million if there is roughly 1 per 5000 residents.

explanations.A: About 20 would imply 1 per 50,000 residents.
explanations.B: About 2000 would imply 1 per 500 residents, far too dense.
explanations.C: Correct. 1,000,000 / 5000 = 200 restaurants.
explanations.D: About 50 implies 1 per 20,000 residents, too sparse.

questions.question: A stick of length 1 is broken at a uniformly random point. What is the expected length of the longer piece?

explanations.A: Correct. E[max(X, 1-X)] = 2 times the integral of x from 1/2 to 1 = 3/4.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the average length of each piece, not specifically the longer one.
explanations.C: 2/3 does not match the integral for the longer piece.
explanations.D: 1 would be the whole stick, ignoring that it is broken.

questions.question: A cereal box contains 1 of 4 equally likely toys. What is the expected number of boxes needed to collect all 4 distinct toys?

explanations.A: 4 is the minimum possible, not the expected number with repeats.
explanations.B: 10 overshoots the coupon-collector sum for 4 toys.
explanations.C: 16 = 4^2 has no basis here.

questions.question: In a uniformly random permutation of 1..n, what is the expected number of fixed points (positions i holding value i)?

explanations.A: Correct. Each position is a fixed point with probability 1/n; by linearity, E = n x (1/n) = 1.
explanations.B: n would mean every position is fixed, i.e. the identity permutation always.
explanations.C: 0 ignores that fixed points do occur on average.
explanations.D: n/2 has no basis; the expectation is exactly 1 for any n.

questions.question: Two people each roll a fair die. What is P(they roll the same number)?

explanations.A: 1/36 is the chance of one specific matching pair like (3,3).
explanations.B: Correct. The first roll can be anything; the second matches it with probability 1/6.
explanations.C: 1/2 vastly overstates the chance of a match.
explanations.D: 1/12 undercounts; there are 6 matching outcomes out of 36.

questions.question: Three ants sit on the corners of a triangle. Each independently walks along one of the two edges from its corner. What is P(no two ants collide)?

explanations.A: 1/2 overstates it; collisions are avoided only in two of eight cases.
explanations.B: 1/8 counts just one no-collision case, but there are two.
explanations.C: Correct. No collision requires all ants to circulate the same way: 2 of 2^3 = 2/8 = 1/4.
explanations.D: 3/4 inverts the probability; collisions are the common outcome.

questions.question: Estimate how many times the word 'the' appears in a 300-page novel (~300 words per page, about 5% of words are 'the').

explanations.B: About 450 is only 0.5% of the words.
explanations.C: About 45,000 would be half of all words being 'the'.
explanations.D: About 90 ignores the per-page word count entirely.

questions.question: You roll a fair die repeatedly. What is P(you see a 6 before you see a 1)?

explanations.A: 1/6 is the chance of a 6 on a single roll, not the race against the 1.
explanations.B: Correct. By symmetry, 6 and 1 are equally likely to appear first, so 1/2.
explanations.C: 1/3 has no basis for this symmetric two-outcome race.
explanations.D: 5/6 confuses 'not a 1 on one roll' with the long-run race.

questions.question: You roll a fair die. You may keep the value or reroll once and must take the second roll. Playing optimally, what is the expected value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the value of never using the reroll option.
explanations.B: 4.0 does not match the optimal keep-or-reroll calculation.
explanations.C: 5.0 is the average of {4,5,6} alone, ignoring the reroll cases.
explanations.D: Correct. Keep 4-6 (mean 5), reroll on 1-3 (then expect 3.5): 0.5 x 5 + 0.5 x 3.5 = 4.25.

questions.question: Roughly how many people must be in a room before it is more likely than not that someone shares YOUR specific birthday?

explanations.A: Correct. Solving 1 - (364/365)^n > 0.5 gives n about 253.
explanations.B: About 23 is the answer for ANY shared pair, not a match to your specific date.
explanations.C: About 183 is a common but incorrect 'half of 365' guess.
explanations.D: 366 guarantees a pair shares some birthday, but not specifically yours.

questions.question: The mass of a typical adult human is closest to:

explanations.A: 7 kg is about the mass of a house cat.
explanations.B: Correct. A typical adult is roughly 70 kg.
explanations.C: 700 kg is closer to a large horse.
explanations.D: 7000 kg is about the mass of an elephant.

questions.question: A bag has 1 red and 1 blue marble. You draw, replace, and repeat 3 times. What is P(exactly 2 reds)?

explanations.A: 1/2 ignores the binomial structure of three draws.
explanations.B: 1/8 is the chance of one specific sequence, not all with two reds.
explanations.D: 3/4 overcounts the favorable sequences.

questions.question: A martingale strategy doubles the bet after each loss on a fair coin. Each completed cycle nets +$1, yet it fails in practice mainly because:
options: a finite bankroll makes a rare long losing streak catastrophic
the expected value of each bet is negative

explanations.A: Correct. Required bets grow exponentially, so a long losing run exhausts any real bankroll before the guaranteed $1 arrives.
explanations.B: The fatal flaw exists even with a perfectly fair coin.
explanations.C: On a fair coin each bet has zero expected value, not negative.
explanations.D: Each cycle does win $1 when it completes; the problem is the rare ruinous streak, not certain loss.

questions.question: Estimate the number of red blood cells in an adult body (~5 million per microliter, ~5 liters of blood). Which order of magnitude is best?

explanations.A: About 10^6 is the count in just one microliter, not the whole body.
explanations.B: Correct. 5 x 10^6 cells/uL x 5 x 10^6 uL = 2.5 x 10^13.
explanations.C: About 10^9 undercounts by roughly four orders of magnitude.
explanations.D: About 10^20 overshoots the body's cell count enormously.
